headlines.title: Police activity shuts down eastbound I-580 in Livermore
headlines.description: Police activity had shut down both directions of Interstate 580, east of Isabel Avenue, in Livermore, according to the California Highway Patrol. CHP Dublin on Friday afternoon reported just before 3 p.m. both directions of the freeway were still shut down. Just before 3:30 p.m., CHP announced only westbound lanes were opened. Just before 4 p.m. CHP said all lanes were reopened. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Deadly shooting near scene of Santa Clara house fire
headlines.description: Santa Clara police on Friday were searching a neighborhood for a suspect who shot and killed a man before possibly setting a house on fire. According to initial police communications, someone reported hearing about eight or nine gunshots at about 12:30 p.m. in the area of Serra and Enright avenues. Officers were heard saying they believed a man was walking to his car, possibly from a house, when another man shot him multiple times. Shortly thereafter, the house went up in flames. Firefighters used ladder trucks to douse the burning structure from above. Neighbors who said they knew the victim were horrified. “It’s horrible, it’s horrible,” Virginia Rogers said. “My neighbor’s husband got killed. They’re a very close family. It’s very, very sad. For her to find out, come home, and find your husband dead on the lawn, it’s horrible.” A resident who owns the house directly behind the burning home called the scene hectic. “I could see the smoke over my roof line,” the resident said. “The police had pulled up and had weapons drawn and ordered my wife and my daughter to evacuate the house, which they did pretty, pretty quickly.” The resident said his wife heard multiple gunshots. As of late Friday afternoon, police were searching homes in the area for the shooter. They plan to enter the house that caught fire to conduct a search there. Police have yet to release any official updates regarding the incident. Several streets in the area were blocked off. No other information was immediately available. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Low clouds expected to cause flight delays at SFO
headlines.description: A ground delay program released by the Federal Aviation Administration on Friday morning is expected to cause major delays for hundreds of flights at San Francisco International Airport throughout the day. A low cloud ceiling at SFO prompted the FAA to delay flights departing and arriving from the airport between 8 a.m. and 11 p.m. Friday, with disruptions expected to average 70 minutes. The program will affect all flights flying to and from San Francisco in the contiguous United States and select Canadian cities, with minimal impact expected on arriving international flights. Flights destined for SFO will delay their departures, especially those within California, to comply with the policy of only 36 arrivals an hour. Decreased visibility due to low clouds requires more aircraft to have more separation when in the air, to ensure the safety of each airplane. Two of the airport’s runways are undergoing maintenance, limiting the available runways for takeoffs and landings. An airport spokesperson said over 200 flights were already experiencing delays as of 11 a.m., while more are expected throughout the day. Flight tracking website FlightAware reported the average delay at SFO was an hour and 15 minutes. United, Southwest, and SkyWest were the airlines with the most delays reported so far. Oakland San Francisco Bay Airport and San Jose Mineta International Airport are each experiencing 22 delays, according to FlightAware. Neither airport, however, has received a ground delay program advisory from the FAA. Travelers are advised to check with their respective airlines to see how their itineraries may have been impacted.
headlines.source: Andres Jimenez Larios | Bay City News

headlines.title: Google settles racial discrimination lawsuit for $50 million
headlines.description: Google has settled with Black employees who alleged systemic racial disparities in hiring, pay, and advancement in a lawsuit filed in 2022. April Curley, a former Google employee, had sued the tech giant for racial discrimination, saying it engages in a “pattern and practice” of unfair treatment for its Black workers. The suit claimed the company steered them into lower-level and lower-paid jobs and subjected them to a hostile work environment if they speak out. Other former Google workers also joined the suit, which later received class action status. “This case is about accountability, plain and simple,” said civil rights attorney Ben Crump, who represented the plaintiffs, in a statement. “For far too long, Black employees in the tech industry have faced barriers that limit opportunity. This settlement is a significant step toward holding one of the world’s most powerful companies accountable and making clear that discriminatory practices cannot and will not be tolerated.” The settlement was announced in May 2025 and granted final approval this week. Google said when the settlement was reached that it strongly disagrees with the allegations that it treated anyone improperly and remains “committed to paying, hiring, and leveling all employees consistently.” The lawsuit, echoed years of complaints from Black employees at the company. That includes prominent artificial intelligence scholar Timnit Gebru, who said she was pushed out in 2020 after a dispute over a research paper examining the societal dangers of an emerging branch of artificial intelligence. The 2022 lawsuit claimed that Mountain View, California-based Google viewed Black job candidates “through harmful racial stereotypes” and claimed that hiring managers deemed Black candidates “not ‘Googly’ enough, a plain dog whistle for race discrimination.” In addition, according to the suit, interviewers “hazed” and undermined Black candidates and hired Black candidates into lower-paying and lower-level roles with less advancement potential based on their race and racial stereotypes. The settlement, which does not constitute admission of liability by Google, also includes a commitment to pay equity analyses, pay transparency measures, and limits on mandatory arbitration for employment-related disputes through at least August 2026, according to Crump.

headlines.title: Canvas system is online after cyberattack disrupted thousands of schools
headlines.description: Tens of thousands of students studying for final exams around the world Friday regained access to a key online learning system after a cyberattack knocked it offline, throwing schools and universities into chaos. A hacking group called ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach at Canvas, said Luke Connolly, a threat analyst at the cybersecurity firm Emsisoft. Instructure, the company behind Canvas, said in an update late Thursday that the system was available for most users. Canvas is used to manage grades, course notes, assignments, lecture videos and more. The hacking group posted online that nearly 9,000 schools worldwide were affected, with billions of private messages and other records accessed, Connolly said. Screenshots Connolly provided showed that the group had been threatening to leak the trove of data. By Friday, Instructure and Canvas had been removed from a dedicated leak site created by the ransomware group on the dark web to publish stolen data, he said. Canvas went down Thursday at the worst possible time, which came as no surprise to Huseyin Can Yuceel, the security research lead at Picus Labs. “Timing is everything, because they want to inflict pain as much as possible,” he said, “so they can extort money out of it.” Colleges & Universities May 7 Education 17 hours ago Rich in digitized data, the nation’s schools are prime targets for far-flung criminal hackers, who are assiduously locating and scooping up sensitive files that not long ago were committed to paper in locked cabinets. Past attacks have hit Minneapolis Public Schools and the Los Angeles Unified School District. Instructure has not posted about the attack on its social media. The company didn’t immediately respond to emails from The Associated Press asking whether it paid a ransom and inquiring about what happened with the compromised data. Students quickly took to social media, with many panicking that they could no longer view course materials housed within the platform to study for their final exams. Teachers said they were having to find workarounds to help students study for exams and submit final assignments. And some schools, such as the University of Texas at San Antonio, announced they were pushing back finals scheduled for Friday in response to the outage. Schools like Princeton University turned to the social platform X late Thursday to announce that Canvas appeared to be working and information technology staff was monitoring the situation. Rhongho Jang, a computer science professor at Wayne State University in Detroit, was finalizing grades for a class of 94 students when the system went down. He keeps paper copies of the student exams, but all of the semester assignments were done online. “That’s 50% weight for the final grading,” Jang said. If those assignments and grades could not be recovered, Jang would have given his students full credit. “I didn’t want to penalize them,” he said. “We cannot judge based on the data we don’t have. The final responsibility is still on the server.” Although he was sure the data was saved and would be recovered, Jang was relieved when Canvas came back up. Allan Liska, of the cybersecurity firm Recorded Future, said the outage did appear deliberate, not a glitch, and that Instructure was trying to figure out how widespread the problem was and make sure the hackers were no longer inside its system. “There’s no indication at this point that any ransom has been paid,” Liska said. “And it likely is still a little too early for a ransom to have been paid. You know, normally these negotiations kind of drag on for a while.” Liska said nothing big has been leaked yet, but said that is common. “Once they’ve leaked, they’ve lost their leverage.” Connolly said the Canvas attack is strikingly similar to a breach at PowerSchool, which also offers learning management tools. In that case, a Massachusetts college student was charged. Connolly described ShinyHunters as a loose affiliation of teenagers and young adults based in the U.S. and the United Kingdom. The group also has been tied to other attacks, including Live Nation’s Ticketmaster subsidiary. ShinyHunters, or an offshoot, also was behind a previous smaller breach of Instructure, Liska said. He added that the group or someone pretending to be ShinyHunters issued a statement Friday indicating that it had nothing to say. “It’s very weird,” Liska said, noting that the group is “normally a very talkative bunch.”

headlines.title: 2 Oakland police officers, suspect injured after traffic stop ends in shooting
headlines.description: Two Oakland police officers and a suspect ended up in the hospital Friday following a traffic stop in which the suspect tried to flee and an officer opened fire, police said. The incident played out at about 3:15 a.m. along the 1200 block of 9th Avenue, according to police. While conducting a traffic stop, officers spotted a firearm in the vehicle, police said. The suspect, a 21-year-old man from Sacramento, proceeded to flee, dragging one officer and pinning the other between the suspect’s vehicle and a parked car. The pinned officer fired his weapon, hitting the suspect once. “Use of force is something we never want to take lightly or resort to, but our officers have the right to defend themselves,” interim Oakland police Chief James Beere said. Both officers were taken to a local hospital and later released, police said. The suspect was hospitalized in stable condition. The police department would not provide information on the reason for the traffic stop, citing the ongoing investigation. They also did not release the names of the suspect and the officers. Investigators said the gun recovered at the scene was stolen. They will work with federal partners to determine why the suspect was in the area. “I can’t get into specifically the intention of why the subject was there,” Beere said. “I can tell you that the neighborhood has experienced violence related to human trafficking and prostitution activity.” Both officers will be placed on paid administrative leave, in accordance with the police department’s policy. The police department, Alameda County District Attorney’s Office and the Community Police Review Agency will conduct independent investigations into the incident. “The Oakland Police Department remains committed to transparency and understands community concerns,” the police department said in a statement. “To ensure the investigation’s integrity, information is limited at this time. More details will be shared as soon as the investigation allows, and in compliance with the law.” This marks the second officer-involved shooting in Oakland in just over a week. Last week, police shot and killed a man they say was pointing a gun at others.

headlines.title: San Leandro police fatally shoot man during welfare check
headlines.description: A man was shot and killed Thursday night after allegedly pointing a firearm at officers during a welfare check at the San Leandro Marina, according to police. The San Leandro Police Department said officers responded at 8:07 p.m. after receiving a request to check on a man who had reportedly made suicidal statements to a friend and was said to be suffering from depression. Officers first searched for the man at a residence in the 14500 block of Santiago Road, but later found him sitting inside a vehicle near the boat launch at the San Leandro Marina, police said. They spoke with the man for several minutes while he stayed inside the vehicle. During the encounter, the man took out a firearm and pointed it at officers, leading to the shooting, police alleged. Emergency medical personnel treated the man at the scene, but he was pronounced dead. Police said a firearm was recovered at the scene. Three officers were involved in the shooting, and none were injured, the department said. Police said they will conduct criminal and administrative investigations into the shooting, while the Alameda County District Attorney’s Office will conduct a concurrent probe. The involved officers will be placed on administrative leave under department policy. “While SLPD remains committed to transparency, the release of additional information related to this incident will be limited at this time to protect the integrity of the ongoing investigation and in accordance with applicable laws,” the department said.

headlines.title: California to provide free diapers to newborns at more than 60 hospitals
headlines.description: California families welcoming newborns will soon receive hundreds of free diapers before leaving the hospital under a first-in-the-nation program announced Friday by Gov. Gavin Newsom. During the program’s first year, it will be offered at about 65 to 75 hospitals that handle about a quarter of births in the state and largely serve low-income patients, Newsom’s office said. The initiative will expand to more hospitals statewide, though the governor’s office did not say how many. The state has partnered with nonprofit Baby2Baby to manufacture the diapers under the label “Golden State Start.” Newsom said the plan builds on California’s effort to make living in the notoriously expensive state a little easier for families. He highlighted policies in recent years to provide students with free meals at school and make preschool free for all. “Every baby born in California deserves a healthy start in life — and that means making sure parents have the basics they need from day one,” the Democrat said in a statement. The state set aside $7.4 million in last year’s budget to roll out the initiative, and this year’s budget proposal includes an additional $12.5 million to implement the program for the upcoming fiscal year ending in June 2027. It will allow each family to receive 400 diapers in sizes for newborns and babies up to 14 pounds (6 kilograms) when they are discharged from a hospital after birth. That’s a little more than a month’s worth, since newborns on average require eight to 10 diapers a day. Baby2Baby provides families in need with essential items. The nonprofit built a manufacturing system that allows them to produce diapers for 80% less than the retail price, co-CEO Kelly Sawyer Patricof said. “California is once again setting the standard of what it means to show up for mothers and babies,” added co-CEO Norah Weinstein. The announcement comes two years after Tennessee and Delaware became the first U.S. states to offer free diapers to families enrolled in their Medicaid programs, which provide healthcare to low-income families. Tennessee families can go to pharmacies to pick up 100 diapers per month for children under two. The Delaware program, which began as a pilot before the state extended it in 2024, provides individuals with up to 80 diapers and up to one pack of baby wipes per week in the first 12 weeks of life. California’s Medicaid system doesn’t cover diapers for newborns, but it does cover the items for enrollees ages 5 and older if they have a medical need for them. Diapers are a large, ongoing expense for many families. They typically spend about $100 a month on the items per child, according to the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities, a liberal think tank. The cost burden can lead some parents to leave their child’s diapers on for too long or reuse disposable diapers, which can result in rashes and urinary tract infections, the center says. California officials are touting the new effort as a way to ease that financial strain. “The first days at home with a newborn should be focused on the love, connection, and joy of an expanded family, not stress about affording diapers,” said Kim Johnson, the state’s health secretary. “This program helps ensure families can begin that journey with greater stability and peace of mind.”

headlines.title: Highway 1 (19th Avenue) southbound closure in San Francisco
headlines.description: A weekend-long road closure is underway in San Francisco as Caltrans begins the second phase of a rehabilitation project on 19th Avenue. Crews started work early Friday morning on the major roadway, which serves as a key artery through the city. This phase focuses on the southbound direction. Two of the three southbound lanes between Lincoln Way and Sloat Boulevard are closed. Earlier this month, Caltrans work centered on the northbound side. San Francisco Apr 27 San Francisco Apr 23 San Francisco Apr 24 The timing is also different for this closure. Crews began work at 3 a.m., earlier than the 7 a.m. start during the first phase, which could impact commutes sooner in the day. Drivers are also being advised of additional closures. From 3 a.m. to 7 a.m. through the weekend, Lincoln Way at 19th Avenue will be closed, with detours along Irving Street. This is the second of three planned weekend closures for the project. Another closure is scheduled for Memorial Day weekend. Parking along 19th Avenue will also be restricted in work zones.

headlines.title: San Francisco firefighter gets court diversion in alleged off-duty attack
headlines.description: A veteran San Francisco firefighter facing misdemeanor charges for an alleged off-duty attack on a patron in a North Beach restaurant will undergo court-ordered diversion in lieu of prosecution, officials said Thursday. Eigil Qwist remains under investigation by the City Attorney’s office for the off-duty incident last October that triggered a civil suit against him and the second in command of the fire department, Patrick Rabbitt. According to the lawsuit and the police reports in the case, Qwist struck a part-time Marin County sheriff’s deputy from behind on Oct. 12, knocking him to the ground. The victim lost his shoe and glasses when he was knocked to the ground, according to investigators who said the incident was captured on security surveillance video. The attack, the civil suit alleges, occurred after the victim, David Gallegioni, confronted firefighters over allegations his daughter made against one of them. San Francisco Mar 20 San Francisco Apr 7 San Francisco May 1 Even though the Gallegioni lawsuit names him as a participant, police said in court documents that they considered Rabbitt to be a witness to the incident at Original Joe’s restaurant. He identified Qwist as involved based on the video footage. Prosecutors, in a statement, announced the resolution of the case. “The San Francisco District Attorney’s Office is committed to fair and ethical prosecutions in all cases,” prosecutors said. “We charge cases based on the facts, evidence, and the law. In this case, Mr. Quist was charged with a statutorily diversion-eligible misdemeanor case, and we had no grounds to object. We object to diversion in cases where we believe diversion is not suitable, for example, in cases where a defendant may have other cases pending or has been previously convicted. In all cases, we consider the individual’s conduct, regardless of who they are.” In March, San Francisco’s fire chief Dean Crispen issued an extraordinary bulletin to firefighters, urging them to “fully” cooperate with two ongoing probes into an “alleged incident.” “The City Attorney’s Office and other independent law enforcement agencies are conducting investigations into the alleged incident,” the chief wrote in a March 17 bulletin. “The Department is fully cooperating with those investigations, and I encourage all of you to do so as well.” Gallegioni’s legal filings assert that he was summoned to the bar that afternoon by his daughter and found himself confronting off-duty firefighters over something she said had happened to her at the bar. Gallegioni said in both the suit and the notice of claim that members of the group wore T-shirts with SFFD logos. The legal claim says that after Gallegioni demanded answers about the allegations, one firefighter talking to his family pointed to others in his group and warned: “Don’t mess with us.” The claim alleges “the statement and gesture conveyed,” that city firefighters “were invoking their affiliation” with the department “as a show of authority and intimidation.” The civil suit names Rabbitt as being part of the attack. But the police statement of probable cause accompanying the original criminal charges designates Rabbitt as a witness. Rabbitt acknowledged to investigators being in the bar that day to watch the 49ers game, but told them he didn’t see anyone being attacked. As for Qwist, he told investigators that was “a mutual thing,” according to the probable cause statement.

headlines.title: Berkeley renews Flock camera contract, rejects expansion plan
headlines.description: Berkeley city leaders Thursday night voted to renew the city’s contract with Flock Safety license plate reader cameras, but rejected a plan to expand the system. The City Council voted late to approve a 12-month contract renewal for the cameras already in use across the city, at a cost of about $200,000. The measure passed narrowly in a 5-4 vote. Councilmembers, however, rejected a proposal to expand the system in a separate 8-1 vote. The decision follows months of debate over whether Berkeley should continue using the technology ahead of the contract’s expiration in July. Berkeley police said the cameras help reduce crime, especially as the department faces staffing shortages. But critics raised concerns about privacy and how the data could be used, including whether it could be shared with federal agencies such as U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). “There’s no reason for Berkeley in this climate with surveillance and ICE and everything that’s going on,” said Berkeley resident Cheryl Davila. A spokesperson for Flock Safety told councilmembers the company has changed its system to prevent data from being shared outside of California. “We shifted our entire business model. It’s not possible for any agency to share information outside of the state,” the spokesperson said. Many speakers at the meeting opposed renewing the contract, citing concerns about surveillance and Berkeley’s status as a sanctuary city. Earlier this week, nearby El Cerrito decided not to renew its own contract with Flock. Berkeley 17 hours ago Oakland Apr 28 Alameda County Apr 22 Alameda County board approves Flock Safety camera extension

headlines.title: Some passengers on the cruise with hantavirus outbreak are back in California
headlines.description: The California Department of Public Health said it has been notified by the CDC of California residents who were on the cruise ship near South America that had passengers infected with the hantavirus. “We are coordinating with local health officials, as needed, to monitor returning travelers. There is no information that the California residents are ill or infected,” a department spokesperson said, adding that it can’t share the travelers’ residences. “At this time, the risk to public health in California is low,” the spokesperson said. This comes as news spreads that at least seven people who were on that cruise have now returned to the United States. The hantavirus has killed three people and sickened five others on the cruise ship. Health May 7 Health May 6 Health May 6 The ship has continued sailing with its remaining passengers. Stephen Kornfeld, a doctor from Oregon, is a passenger on the cruise ship. He said that when the ship’s doctor fell ill, he began helping out by treating patients on board. “I just sort of fell into the role of ship physician as people deteriorated and more people got sick,” he said. Dr. Abraar Karan, an infectious disease faculty and attending physician at Stanford, explained that Hantavirus is most commonly spread from rodents to humans. “Typically, you need to be near either urine or feces of a rodent that is infected, and then you would need to be breathing that in in a very closed space,” he said. He noted that the Andes strain of the Hantavirus that was detected on the cruise ship is the only strain that is known to spread between humans. The Andes strain is typically found in South America. Karan said that, unlike COVID-19, he does not believe there will be widespread public transmission of Hantavirus. “The risk is extremely low of anyone in California coming into contact with– you know — the return travelers who’ve come back here, particularly because it’s been a very coordinated effort with the California Department of Public Health,” Karan said, noting that public health officials will be tracking the impacted passengers from this cruise.
